     2	import Value from './value';
     3	import Selector from './selector';
     4	import AtRule from './atrule';
     5	import NestableAtRulePrototype from './nested-at-rule';
     6	
     7	const Media = function(value, features, index, currentFileInfo, visibilityInfo) {
     8	    this._index = index;
     9	    this._fileInfo = currentFileInfo;
    10	
    11	    const selectors = (new Selector([], null, null, this._index, this._fileInfo)).createEmptySelectors();
    12	
    13	    this.features = new Value(features);
    14	    this.rules = [new Ruleset(selectors, value)];
    15	    this.rules[0].allowImports = true;
    16	    this.copyVisibilityInfo(visibilityInfo);
    17	    this.allowRoot = true;
    18	    this.setParent(selectors, this);
    19	    this.setParent(this.features, this);
    20	    this.setParent(this.rules, this);
    21	};
    22	
    23	Media.prototype = Object.assign(new AtRule(), {
    24	    type: 'Media',
    25	
    26	    ...NestableAtRulePrototype,
    27	
    28	    genCSS(context, output) {
    29	        output.add('@media ', this._fileInfo, this._index);
    30	        this.features.genCSS(context, output);
    31	        this.outputRuleset(context, output, this.rules);
    32	    },
    33	
    34	    eval(context) {
    35	        if (!context.mediaBlocks) {
    36	            context.mediaBlocks = [];
    37	            context.mediaPath = [];
    38	        }
    39	
    40	        const media = new Media(null, [], this._index, this._fileInfo, this.visibilityInfo());
    41	        if (this.debugInfo) {
    42	            this.rules[0].debugInfo = this.debugInfo;
    43	            media.debugInfo = this.debugInfo;
    44	        }
    45	        
    46	        media.features = this.features.eval(context);
    47	
    48	        context.mediaPath.push(media);
    49	        context.mediaBlocks.push(media);
    50	
    51	        this.rules[0].functionRegistry = context.frames[0].functionRegistry.inherit();
    52	        context.frames.unshift(this.rules[0]);
    53	        media.rules = [this.rules[0].eval(context)];
    54	        context.frames.shift();
    55	
    56	        context.mediaPath.pop();
    57	
    58	        return context.mediaPath.length === 0 ? media.evalTop(context) :
    59	            media.evalNested(context);
    60	    }
    61	});
    62	
    63	export default Media;

     1	import { copy } from 'copy-anything';
     2	import Declaration from './declaration';
     3	import Node from './node';
     4	
     5	const QueryInParens = function (op, l, m, op2, r, i) {
     6	    this.op = op.trim();
     7	    this.lvalue = l;
     8	    this.mvalue = m;
     9	    this.op2 = op2 ? op2.trim() : null;
    10	    this.rvalue = r;
    11	    this._index = i;
    12	    this.mvalues = [];
    13	};
    14	
    15	QueryInParens.prototype = Object.assign(new Node(), {
    16	    type: 'QueryInParens',
    17	
    18	    accept(visitor) {
    19	        this.lvalue = visitor.visit(this.lvalue);
    20	        this.mvalue = visitor.visit(this.mvalue);
    21	        if (this.rvalue) {
    22	            this.rvalue = visitor.visit(this.rvalue);
    23	        }
    24	    },
    25	
    26	    eval(context) {
    27	        this.lvalue = this.lvalue.eval(context);
    28	        
    29	        let variableDeclaration;
    30	        let rule;
    31	
    32	        for (let i = 0; (rule = context.frames[i]); i++) {
    33	            if (rule.type === 'Ruleset') {
    34	                variableDeclaration = rule.rules.find(function (r) {
    35	                    if ((r instanceof Declaration) && r.variable) {
    36	                        return true;
    37	                    }
    38	
    39	                    return false;
    40	                });
    41	                
    42	                if (variableDeclaration) {
    43	                    break;
    44	                }
    45	            }
    46	        }
    47	
    48	        if (!this.mvalueCopy) {
    49	            this.mvalueCopy = copy(this.mvalue);
    50	        }
    51	        
    52	        if (variableDeclaration) {
    53	            this.mvalue = this.mvalueCopy;
    54	            this.mvalue = this.mvalue.eval(context);
    55	            this.mvalues.push(this.mvalue);
    56	        } else {
    57	            this.mvalue = this.mvalue.eval(context);
    58	        }
    59	
    60	        if (this.rvalue) {
    61	            this.rvalue = this.rvalue.eval(context);
    62	        }
    63	        return this;
    64	    },
    65	
    66	    genCSS(context, output) {
    67	        this.lvalue.genCSS(context, output);
    68	        output.add(' ' + this.op + ' ');
    69	        if (this.mvalues.length > 0) {
    70	            this.mvalue = this.mvalues.shift();
    71	        }
    72	        this.mvalue.genCSS(context, output);
    73	        if (this.rvalue) {
    74	            output.add(' ' + this.op2 + ' ');
    75	            this.rvalue.genCSS(context, output);
    76	        }
    77	    },
    78	});
    79	
    80	export default QueryInParens;
    81

2026-05-30 13:14:44,042 - DEBUG - swea-tools-less__less.js-4363 - Retrieved state from environment: {'working_dir': '/testbed'}
2026-05-30 13:14:44,044 - INFO - swea-agent-less__less.js-4363 - 🤖 MODEL INPUT
OBSERVATION:

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /testbed/packages/less/src/less/parser/parser.js:
     1	import LessError from '../less-error';
     2	import tree from '../tree';
     3	import visitors from '../visitors';
     4	import getParserInput from './parser-input';
     5	import * as utils from '../utils';
     6	import functionRegistry from '../functions/function-registry';
     7	import { ContainerSyntaxOptions, MediaSyntaxOptions } from '../tree/atrule-syntax';
     8	import logger from '../logger';
     9	import Selector from '../tree/selector';
    10	import Anonymous from '../tree/anonymous';
    11	
    12	//
    13	// less.js - parser
    14	//
    15	//    A relatively straight-forward predictive parser.
    16	//    There is no tokenization/lexing stage, the input is parsed
    17	//    in one sweep.
    18	//
    19	//    To make the parser fast enough to run in the browser, several
    20	//    optimization had to be made:
    21	//
    22	//    - Matching and slicing on a huge input is often cause of slowdowns.
    23	//      The solution is to chunkify the input into smaller strings.
    24	//      The chunks are stored in the `chunks` var,
    25	//      `j` holds the current chunk index, and `currentPos` holds
    26	//      the index of the current chunk in relation to `input`.
    27	//      This gives us an almost 4x speed-up.
    28	//
    29	//    - In many cases, we don't need to match individual tokens;
    30	//      for example, if a value doesn't hold any variables, operations
    31	//      or dynamic references, the parser can effectively 'skip' it,
    32	//      treating it as a literal.
    33	//      An example would be '1px solid #000' - which evaluates to itself,
    34	//      we don't need to know what the individual components are.
    35	//      The drawback, of course is that you don't get the benefits of
    36	//      syntax-checking on the CSS. This gives us a 50% speed-up in the parser,
    37	//      and a smaller speed-up in the code-gen.
    38	//
    39	//
    40	//    Token matching is done with the `$` function, which either takes
    41	//    a terminal string or regexp, or a non-terminal function to call.
    42	//    It also takes care of moving all the indices forwards.
    43	//
    44	
    45	const Parser = function Parser(context, imports, fileInfo, currentIndex) {
    46	    currentIndex = currentIndex || 0;
    47	    let parsers;
    48	    const parserInput = getParserInput();
    49	
    50	    function error(msg, type) {
    51	        throw new LessError(
    52	            {
    53	                index: parserInput.i,
    54	                filename: fileInfo.filename,
    55	                type: type || 'Syntax',
    56	                message: msg
    57	            },
    58	            imports
    59	        );
    60	    }
    61	
    62	    /**
    63	     * 
    64	     * @param {string} msg 
    65	     * @param {number} index 
    66	     * @param {string} type 
    67	     */
    68	    function warn(msg, index, type) {
    69	        if (!context.quiet) {
    70	            logger.warn(
    71	                (new LessError(
    72	                    {
    73	                        index: index ?? parserInput.i,
    74	                        filename: fileInfo.filename,
    75	                        type: type ? `${type.toUpperCase()} WARNING` : 'WARNING',
    76	                        message: msg
    77	                    },
    78	                    imports
    79	                )).toString()
    80	            );
    81	        }
    82	    }
    83	
    84	    function expect(arg, msg) {
    85	        // some older browsers return typeof 'function' for RegExp
    86	        const result = (arg instanceof Function) ? arg.call(parsers) : parserInput.$re(arg);
    87	        if (result) {
    88	            return result;
    89	        }
    90	
    91	        error(msg || (typeof arg === 'string'
    92	            ? `expected '${arg}' got '${parserInput.currentChar()}'`
    93	            : 'unexpected token'));
    94	    }
    95	
    96	    // Specialization of expect()
    97	    function expectChar(arg, msg) {
    98	        if (parserInput.$char(arg)) {
    99	            return arg;
   100	        }
   101	        error(msg || `expected '${arg}' got '${parserInput.currentChar()}'`);
   102	    }
   103	
   104	    function getDebugInfo(index) {
   105	        const filename = fileInfo.filename;
   106	
   107	        return {
   108	            lineNumber: utils.getLocation(index, parserInput.getInput()).line + 1,
   109	            fileName: filename
   110	        };
   111	    }
   112	
   113	    /**
   114	     *  Used after initial parsing to create nodes on the fly
   115	     *
   116	     *  @param {String} str          - string to parse
   117	     *  @param {Array}  parseList    - array of parsers to run input through e.g. ["value", "important"]
   118	     *  @param {Number} currentIndex - start number to begin indexing
   119	     *  @param {Object} fileInfo     - fileInfo to attach to created nodes
   120	     */
   121	    function parseNode(str, parseList, callback) {
   122	        let result;
   123	        const returnNodes = [];
   124	        const parser = parserInput;
   125	
   126	        try {
   127	            parser.start(str, false, function fail(msg, index) {
   128	                callback({
   129	                    message: msg,
   130	                    index: index + currentIndex
   131	                });
   132	            });
   133	            for (let x = 0, p; (p = parseList[x]); x++) {
   134	                result = parsers[p]();
   135	                returnNodes.push(result || null);
   136	            }
   137	
   138	            const endInfo = parser.end();
   139	            if (endInfo.isFinished) {
   140	                callback(null, returnNodes);
   141	            }
   142	            else {
   143	                callback(true, null);
   144	            }
   145	        } catch (e) {
   146	            throw new LessError({
   147	                index: e.index + currentIndex,
   148	                message: e.message
   149	            }, imports, fileInfo.filename);
   150	        }
   151	    }
   152	
   153	    //
   154	    // The Parser
   155	    //
   156	    return {
   157	        parserInput,
   158	        imports,
   159	        fileInfo,
   160	        parseNode,
   161	        //
   162	        // Parse an input string into an abstract syntax tree,
   163	        // @param str A string containing 'less' markup
   164	        // @param callback call `callback` when done.
   165	        // @param [additionalData] An optional map which can contains vars - a map (key, value) of variables to apply
   166	        //
   167	        parse: function (str, callback, additionalData) {
   168	            let root;
   169	            let err = null;
   170	            let globalVars;
   171	            let modifyVars;
   172	            let ignored;
   173	            let preText = '';
   174	
   175	            // Optionally disable @plugin parsing
   176	            if (additionalData && additionalData.disablePluginRule) {
   177	                parsers.plugin = function() {
   178	                    var dir = parserInput.$re(/^@plugin?\s+/);
   179	                    if (dir) {
   180	                        error('@plugin statements are not allowed when disablePluginRule is set to true');
   181	                    }
   182	                }
   183	            }
   184	
   185	            globalVars = (additionalData && additionalData.globalVars) ? `${Parser.serializeVars(additionalData.globalVars)}\n` : '';
   186	            modifyVars = (additionalData && additionalData.modifyVars) ? `\n${Parser.serializeVars(additionalData.modifyVars)}` : '';
   187	
   188	            if (context.pluginManager) {
   189	                const preProcessors = context.pluginManager.getPreProcessors();
   190	                for (let i = 0; i < preProcessors.length; i++) {
   191	                    str = preProcessors[i].process(str, { context, imports, fileInfo });
   192	                }
   193	            }
   194	
   195	            if (globalVars || (additionalData && additionalData.banner)) {
   196	                preText = ((additionalData && additionalData.banner) ? additionalData.banner : '') + globalVars;
   197	                ignored = imports.contentsIgnoredChars;
   198	                ignored[fileInfo.filename] = ignored[fileInfo.filename] || 0;
   199	                ignored[fileInfo.filename] += preText.length;
   200	            }
   201	
   202	            str = str.replace(/\r\n?/g, '\n');
   203	            // Remove potential UTF Byte Order Mark
   204	            str = preText + str.replace(/^\uFEFF/, '') + modifyVars;
   205	            imports.contents[fileInfo.filename] = str;
   206	
   207	            // Start with the primary rule.
   208	            // The whole syntax tree is held under a Ruleset node,
   209	            // with the `root` property set to true, so no `{}` are
   210	            // output. The callback is called when the input is parsed.
   211	            try {
   212	                parserInput.start(str, context.chunkInput, function fail(msg, index) {
   213	                    throw new LessError({
   214	                        index,
   215	                        type: 'Parse',
   216	                        message: msg,
   217	                        filename: fileInfo.filename
   218	                    }, imports);
   219	                });
   220	
   221	                tree.Node.prototype.parse = this;
   222	                root = new tree.Ruleset(null, this.parsers.primary());
   223	                tree.Node.prototype.rootNode = root;
   224	                root.root = true;
   225	                root.firstRoot = true;
   226	                root.functionRegistry = functionRegistry.inherit();
   227	
   228	            } catch (e) {
   229	                return callback(new LessError(e, imports, fileInfo.filename));
   230	            }
   231	
   232	            // If `i` is smaller than the `input.length - 1`,
   233	            // it means the parser wasn't able to parse the whole
   234	            // string, so we've got a parsing error.
   235	            //
   236	            // We try to extract a \n delimited string,
   237	            // showing the line where the parse error occurred.
   238	            // We split it up into two parts (the part which parsed,
   239	            // and the part which didn't), so we can color them differently.
   240	            const endInfo = parserInput.end();
   241	            if (!endInfo.isFinished) {
   242	
   243	                let message = endInfo.furthestPossibleErrorMessage;
   244	
   245	                if (!message) {
   246	                    message = 'Unrecognised input';
   247	                    if (endInfo.furthestChar === '}') {
   248	                        message += '. Possibly missing opening \'{\'';
   249	                    } else if (endInfo.furthestChar === ')') {
   250	                        message += '. Possibly missing opening \'(\'';
   251	                    } else if (endInfo.furthestReachedEnd) {
   252	                        message += '. Possibly missing something';
   253	                    }
   254	                }
   255	
   256	                err = new LessError({
   257	                    type: 'Parse',
   258	                    message,
   259	                    index: endInfo.furthest,
   260	                    filename: fileInfo.filename
   261	                }, imports);
   262	            }
   263	
   264	            const finish = e => {
   265	                e = err || e || imports.error;
   266	
   267	                if (e) {
   268	                    if (!(e instanceof LessError)) {
   269	                        e = new LessError(e, imports, fileInfo.filename);
   270	                    }
   271	
   272	                    return callback(e);
   273	                }
   274	                else {
   275	                    return callback(null, root);
   276	                }
   277	            };
   278	
   279	            if (context.processImports !== false) {
   280	                new visitors.ImportVisitor(imports, finish)
   281	                    .run(root);
   282	            } else {
   283	                return finish();
   284	            }
   285	        },
   286	
   287	        //
   288	        // Here in, the parsing rules/functions
   289	        //
   290	        // The basic structure of the syntax tree generated is as follows:
   291	        //
   292	        //   Ruleset ->  Declaration -> Value -> Expression -> Entity
   293	        //
   294	        // Here's some Less code:
   295	        //
   296	        //    .class {
   297	        //      color: #fff;
   298	        //      border: 1px solid #000;
   299	        //      width: @w + 4px;
   300	        //      > .child {...}
   301	        //    }
   302	        //
   303	        // And here's what the parse tree might look like:
   304	        //
   305	        //     Ruleset (Selector '.class', [
   306	        //         Declaration ("color",  Value ([Expression [Color #fff]]))
   307	        //         Declaration ("border", Value ([Expression [Dimension 1px][Keyword "solid"][Color #000]]))
   308	        //         Declaration ("width",  Value ([Expression [Operation " + " [Variable "@w"][Dimension 4px]]]))
   309	        //         Ruleset (Selector [Element '>', '.child'], [...])
   310	        //     ])
   311	        //
   312	        //  In general, most rules will try to parse a token with the `$re()` function, and if the return
   313	        //  value is truly, will return a new node, of the relevant type. Sometimes, we need to check
   314	        //  first, before parsing, that's when we use `peek()`.
   315	        //
   316	        parsers: parsers = {
   317	            //
   318	            // The `primary` rule is the *entry* and *exit* point of the parser.
   319	            // The rules here can appear at any level of the parse tree.
   320	            //
   321	            // The recursive nature of the grammar is an interplay between the `block`
   322	            // rule, which represents `{ ... }`, the `ruleset` rule, and this `primary` rule,
   323	            // as represented by this simplified grammar:
   324	            //
   325	            //     primary  →  (ruleset | declaration)+
   326	            //     ruleset  →  selector+ block
   327	            //     block    →  '{' primary '}'
   328	            //
   329	            // Only at one point is the primary rule not called from the
   330	            // block rule: at the root level.
   331	            //
   332	            primary: function () {
   333	                const mixin = this.mixin;
   334	                let root = [];
   335	                let node;
   336	
   337	                while (true) {
   338	                    while (true) {
   339	                        node = this.comment();
   340	                        if (!node) { break; }
   341	                        root.push(node);
   342	                    }
   343	                    // always process comments before deciding if finished
   344	                    if (parserInput.finished) {
   345	                        break;
   346	                    }
   347	                    if (parserInput.peek('}')) {
   348	                        break;
   349	                    }
   350	
   351	                    node = this.extendRule();
   352	                    if (node) {
   353	                        root = root.concat(node);
   354	                        continue;
   355	                    }
   356	
   357	                    node = mixin.definition() || this.declaration() || mixin.call(false, false) ||
   358	                        this.ruleset() || this.variableCall() || this.entities.call() || this.atrule();
   359	                    if (node) {
   360	                        root.push(node);
   361	                    } else {
   362	                        let foundSemiColon = false;
   363	                        while (parserInput.$char(';')) {
   364	                            foundSemiColon = true;
   365	                        }
   366	                        if (!foundSemiColon) {
   367	                            break;
   368	                        }
   369	                    }
   370	                }
   371	
   372	                return root;
   373	            },
   374	
   375	            // comments are collected by the main parsing mechanism and then assigned to nodes
   376	            // where the current structure allows it
   377	            comment: function () {
   378	                if (parserInput.commentStore.length) {
   379	                    const comment = parserInput.commentStore.shift();
   380	                    return new(tree.Comment)(comment.text, comment.isLineComment, comment.index + currentIndex, fileInfo);
   381	                }
   382	            },
   383	
   384	            //
   385	            // Entities are tokens which can be found inside an Expression
   386	            //
   387	            entities: {
   388	                mixinLookup: function() {
   389	                    return parsers.mixin.call(true, true);
   390	                },
   391	                //
   392	                // A string, which supports escaping " and '
   393	                //
   394	                //     "milky way" 'he\'s the one!'
   395	                //
   396	                quoted: function (forceEscaped) {
   397	                    let str;
   398	                    const index = parserInput.i;
   399	                    let isEscaped = false;
   400	
   401	                    parserInput.save();
   402	                    if (parserInput.$char('~')) {
   403	                        isEscaped = true;
   404	                    } else if (forceEscaped) {
   405	                        parserInput.restore();
   406	                        return;
   407	                    }
   408	
   409	                    str = parserInput.$quoted();
   410	                    if (!str) {
   411	                        parserInput.restore();
   412	                        return;
   413	                    }
   414	                    parserInput.forget();
   415	
   416	                    return new(tree.Quoted)(str.charAt(0), str.substr(1, str.length - 2), isEscaped, index + currentIndex, fileInfo);
   417	                },
   418	
   419	                //
   420	                // A catch-all word, such as:
   421	                //
   422	                //     black border-collapse
   423	                //
   424	                keyword: function () {
   425	                    const k = parserInput.$char('%') || parserInput.$re(/^\[?(?:[\w-]|\\(?:[A-Fa-f0-9]<response clipped><NOTE>To save on context only part of this file has been shown to you. You should retry this tool after you have searched inside the file with `grep -n` in order to find the line numbers of what you are looking for.</NOTE>

2026-05-30 13:14:51,260 - INFO - swea-agent-less__less.js-4363 - ========================= STEP 11 =========================
2026-05-30 13:14:51,263 - DEBUG - swea-lm-less__less.js-4363 - n_cache_control: 2
2026-05-30 13:15:04,571 - DEBUG - swea-lm-less__less.js-4363 - Response: ModelResponse(id='d5a65fbe1a6145479051f38d4523cc06', created=1780146896, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content='Now I need to understand the issue. The error message from the PR description shows:\n\n```\n@media print(max-width: 992px) {\n```\n\nThis is invalid CSS - `@media print` is a media query with `print` as a media type, but then `(max-width: 992px)` is directly attached. In proper CSS, this should be:\n\n```css\n@media print and (max-width: 992px) { }\n```\n\nor\n\n```css\n@media print, screen and (max-width: 992px) { }\n```\n\nBut wait, the issue says LESS 4.4.0 worked and 4.4.1 broke. Let me look at what changed. 2026-05-30 13:15:04,575 - INFO - swea-agent-less__less.js-4363 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I need to understand the issue. The error message from the PR description shows:

```
@media print(max-width: 992px) {
```

This is invalid CSS - `@media print` is a media query with `print` as a media type, but then `(max-width: 992px)` is directly attached. In proper CSS, this should be:

```css
@media print and (max-width: 992px) { }
```

or

```css
@media print, screen and (max-width: 992px) { }
```

But wait, the issue says LESS 4.4.0 worked and 4.4.1 broke. Let me look at what changed. Let me check the dist file and the source for changes related to the media feature parsing.

Let me first create a script to reproduce the error

🎬 ACTION
cd /testbed && node -e "
const less = require('./packages/less');
less.render('@media print(max-width: 992px) { .main-view { display: flex; } }', (err, output) => {
    if (err) {
        console.log('ERROR:', err.message);
    } else {
        console.log('SUCCESS:', output.css);
    }
});
"

The `declarationCall` function on line 486 matches `[\\w]+\\(` which would match `print(`.   3723	                        if (!validCall) {
  3724	                            parserInput.forget();
  3725	                            return;
  3726	                        }
  3727	                        validCall = validCall.substring(0, validCall.length - 1);
  3728	                        var rule = this.ruleProperty();
  3729	                        var value;
  3730	                        if (rule) {
  3731	                            value = this.value();
  3732	                        }
  3733	                        if (rule && value) {
  3734	                            args = [new (tree.Declaration)(rule, value, null, null, parserInput.i + currentIndex, fileInfo, true)];
  3735	                        }
  3736	                        if (!parserInput.$char(')')) {
  3737	                            parserInput.restore('Could not parse call arguments or missing \')\'');
  3738	                            return;
  3739	                        }
  3740	                        parserInput.forget();
  3741	                        return new (tree.Call)(validCall, args, index + currentIndex, fileInfo);
  3742	                    },
  3743	                    //
  3744	                    // Parsing rules for functions with non-standard args, e.g.:
  3745	                    //
  3746	                    //     boolean(not(2 > 1))
  3747	                    //
  3748	                    //     This is a quick prototype, to be modified/improved when
  3749	                    //     more custom-parsed funcs come (e.g. `selector(...)`)
  3750	                    //
  3751	                    customFuncCall: function (name) {
  3752	                        /* Ideally the table is to be moved out of here for faster perf.,
  3753	                           but it's quite tricky since it relies on all these `parsers`
  3754	                           and `expect` available only here */
  3755	                        return {
  3756	                            alpha: f(parsers.ieAlpha, true),
  3757	                            boolean: f(condition),
  3758	                            'if': f(condition)
  3759	                        }[name.toLowerCase()];
  3760	                        function f(parse, stop) {

Now I understand the issue. The `declarationCall` on line 3722 matches `^[\w]+\(` which matches `print(`. When parsing `@media print(max-width: 992px)`, the parser:

1. Calls `mediaFeature`
2. First tries `declarationCall` which matches `print(max-width: 992px)` 
3. Inside `declarationCall`, it calls `this.ruleProperty()` which should parse `max-width` as a rule property
4. Then it calls `this.value()` to parse `992px`
5. Then expects `)`

But wait - the issue says it was working in 4.4.0 and broke in 4.4.1. Let me check if there's something about how `declarationCall` was changed. Maybe the issue is about something else.

Let me look at the specific error message from the PR description again: `Unexpected token Function("print")`. This suggests the CSS output has `@media` followed by something that looks like a function call `print(...)` which isn't valid CSS for lightningcss minifier.

Actually, looking at this more carefully, the issue is that `@media print(max-width: 992px)` should be parsed as:
- `print` is a media type 
- `(max-width: 992px)` is a media feature

But currently the parser treats `print(max-width: 992px)` as a declaration/function call, which outputs something like `@media print(max-width: 992px)` in the CSS. This is actually valid LESS but when passed to lightningcss (a CSS minifier), it fails because `print(max-width: 992px)` is not valid CSS media query syntax.

Wait, but the PR says "after upgrading from LESS 4.4.0 to 4.4.1". Let me check if the issue is that in 4.4.0 this was parsed differently. Let me look at the `mediaFeature` function more carefully.

The key issue is: `@media print(max-width: 992px)` - the `print` is a media type and `(max-width: 992px)` is a media feature. But the current parser, when it encounters `print(`, matches it as a `declarationCall` (function call), which creates a `tree.Call` node. This then gets output as `print(max-width: 992px)` in the CSS.

In standard CSS, `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` is valid, but `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is not. The lightningcss minifier chokes on this.

But wait, the PR says this worked in 4.4.0. So maybe the change between 4.4.0 and 4.4.1 introduced the `declarationCall` handling in media features?

Let me look at what could have changed. Let me search for any recent changes related to this:

Actually, let me re-read the source. Looking at line 1896:

```javascript

```

The `declarationCall` is tried first. If it matches `print(max-width: 992px)`, it returns a `Call` node, and `e` is truthy, so it goes to `nodes.push(e)` on line 1898. Then the do-while loop continues because `e` is truthy. But there's no more input, so the loop ends.

5. `entities.keyword()` fails
6. `parserInput.$char('(')` matches `(`
7. Then it goes into the `else if` branch and parses the content inside parens

So the fix would be to not let `declarationCall` match media type names like `print`, `screen`, `all`, etc.

But a better fix: the `declarationCall` function matches `[\w]+\(`. This matches `print(`. But `print` is a media type, not a function. However, LESS can't really know that `print` is a media type vs a function call.

Now let me think about what the actual problem is. The PR says that `@media print` is throwing an error after upgrading from LESS 4.4.0 to 4.4.1. The error is from lightningcss: `Unexpected token Function("print")`.

Looking at the source code, when we parse `@media print(max-width: 992px)`, the `mediaFeature` function is called. It tries `declarationCall` first which matches `print(max-width: 992px)` as a function call. This generates a `Call` node with name `print` and arguments `[Declaration(max-width, 992px)]`. 

But wait, the error says `@media print(max-width: 992px)` - there's no space between `print` and `(`. Let me check: does `declarationCall` require no space between the function name and the parenthesis? Looking at line 486: `parserInput.$re(/^[\w]+\(/)` - this matches word characters directly followed by `(`. So yes, `print(` matches.

But in the PR, the input is `@media print(max-width: 992px)`. This would be parsed as `@media` with features `print(max-width: 992px)` which is a function call. Then the output CSS would be `@media print(max-width: 992px)`. The lightningcss minifier then sees `Function("print")` in the media query and fails.

Now, the question is: what changed between 4.4.0 and 4.4.1 that caused this? Let me look at the `declarationCall` in the `mediaFeature` function. Maybe previously, `declarationCall` wasn't there, and `keyword` would match `print`, then `(` would be matched separately.

Both src and lib have the same logic. Now let me think about what the fix should be.

The problem is that `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is being parsed incorrectly. The `print` is a media type, and `(max-width: 992px)` is a media feature. But the parser sees `print(` and treats it as a function call.

In CSS, the valid syntax would be:
- `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` 
- `@media print, screen and (max-width: 992px)`

But `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is not valid CSS. However, LESS previously (4.4.0) apparently handled this by outputting `@media print(max-width: 992px)` which is also not valid CSS but might have been accepted by some tools.

Wait, let me reconsider. The PR says the error is from lightningcss minify: `Unexpected token Function("print")`. This means that LESS is outputting something like:

```css
@media print(max-width: 992px) {
  .main-view {
    display: flex;
  }
}
```

And lightningcss sees `print(max-width: 992px)` as a function call, which is invalid in CSS media queries.

But the question is: what should the correct output be? In LESS, `@media print(max-width: 992px)` could be interpreted as:
1. A media query with type `print` and feature `(max-width: 992px)` → should output `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` or `@media print (max-width: 992px)`? 
2. Actually, CSS spec says `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` is valid, but `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is not.

But wait, looking at the PR description more carefully, the input is `@media print(max-width: 992px)`. In Less, this could be a typo by the user (forgetting `and`). But the fact that it worked in 4.4.0 and broke in 4.4.1 suggests that something changed in how this is parsed.

     1	import Node from './node';
     2	import Anonymous from './anonymous';
     3	import FunctionCaller from '../functions/function-caller';
     4	
     5	//
     6	// A function call node.
     7	//
     8	const Call = function(name, args, index, currentFileInfo) {
     9	    this.name = name;
    10	    this.args = args;
    11	    this.calc = name === 'calc';
    12	    this._index = index;
    13	    this._fileInfo = currentFileInfo;
    14	}
    15	
    16	Call.prototype = Object.assign(new Node(), {
    17	    type: 'Call',
    18	
    19	    accept(visitor) {
    20	        if (this.args) {
    21	            this.args = visitor.visitArray(this.args);
    22	        }
    23	    },
    24	
    25	    //
    26	    // When evaluating a function call,
    27	    // we either find the function in the functionRegistry,
    28	    // in which case we call it, passing the  evaluated arguments,
    29	    // if this returns null or we cannot find the function, we
    30	    // simply print it out as it appeared originally [2].
    31	    //
    32	    // The reason why we evaluate the arguments, is in the case where
    33	    // we try to pass a variable to a function, like: `saturate(@color)`.
    34	    // The function should receive the value, not the variable.
    35	    //
    36	    eval(context) {
    37	        /**
    38	         * Turn off math for calc(), and switch back on for evaluating nested functions
    39	         */
    40	        const currentMathContext = context.mathOn;
    41	        context.mathOn = !this.calc;
    42	        if (this.calc || context.inCalc) {
    43	            context.enterCalc();
    44	        }
    45	
    46	        const exitCalc = () => {
    47	            if (this.calc || context.inCalc) {
    48	                context.exitCalc();
    49	            }
    50	            context.mathOn = currentMathContext;
    51	        };
    52	
    53	        let result;
    54	        const funcCaller = new FunctionCaller(this.name, context, this.getIndex(), this.fileInfo());
    55	
    56	        if (funcCaller.isValid()) {
    57	            try {
    58	                result = funcCaller.call(this.args);
    59	                exitCalc();
    60	            } catch (e) {
    61	                // eslint-disable-next-line no-prototype-builtins
    62	                if (e.hasOwnProperty('line') && e.hasOwnProperty('column')) {
    63	                    throw e;
    64	                }
    65	                throw { 
    66	                    type: e.type || 'Runtime',
    67	                    message: `Error evaluating function \`${this.name}\`${e.message ? `: ${e.message}` : ''}`,
    68	                    index: this.getIndex(), 
    69	                    filename: this.fileInfo().filename,
    70	                    line: e.lineNumber,
    71	                    column: e.columnNumber
    72	                };
    73	            }
    74	        }
    75	
    76	        if (result !== null && result !== undefined) {
    77	            // Results that that are not nodes are cast as Anonymous nodes
    78	            // Falsy values or booleans are returned as empty nodes
    79	            if (!(result instanceof Node)) {
    80	                if (!result || result === true) {
    81	                    result = new Anonymous(null); 
    82	                }
    83	                else {
    84	                    result = new Anonymous(result.toString()); 
    85	                }
    86	                
    87	            }
    88	            result._index = this._index;
    89	            result._fileInfo = this._fileInfo;
    90	            return result;
    91	        }
    92	
    93	        const args = this.args.map(a => a.eval(context));
    94	        exitCalc();
    95	
    96	        return new Call(this.name, args, this.getIndex(), this.fileInfo());
    97	    },
    98	
    99	    genCSS(context, output) {
   100	        output.add(`${this.name}(`, this.fileInfo(), this.getIndex());
   101	
   102	        for (let i = 0; i < this.args.length; i++) {
   103	            this.args[i].genCSS(context, output);
   104	            if (i + 1 < this.args.length) {
   105	                output.add(', ');
   106	            }
   107	        }
   108	
   109	        output.add(')');
   110	    }
   111	});
   112	
   113	export default Call;
   114

2026-05-30 13:18:17,724 - INFO - swea-agent-less__less.js-4363 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I see the full picture. When `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is parsed:
1. `mediaFeature` tries `declarationCall` which matches `print(max-width: 992px)` and returns a `Call` node
2. This `Call` node is put into the features array
3. When generating CSS, `Call.genCSS` outputs `print(max-width: 992px)`
4. The final CSS is `@media print(max-width: 992px)` which lightningcss can't parse

The fix should be in the `mediaFeature` function. The `declarationCall` should NOT be used for media features because media type names like `print`, `screen`, `all` followed by `(` should be treated as a media type + a parenthesized feature, not as a function call.

Actually, looking at this more carefully, I think the real issue is that `declarationCall` was added to support something like `@media screen and (color)` or custom functions, but it's too aggressive. The fix should be that in `mediaFeature`, we should first try to parse a keyword, and only if that fails (and we see a `word(` pattern), try `declarationCall`.

But wait, looking at line 1896:
```
e = entities.declarationCall.bind(this)() || entities.keyword() || entities.variable() || entities.mixinLookup()
```

The order is: `declarationCall` first, then `keyword`. If we swap them, `keyword` would match `print` first, and then on the next iteration, `(` would be matched. But that would break `declarationCall` entirely in media features.

Actually, maybe the fix is simpler: we should NOT use `declarationCall` in `mediaFeature` at all. The `declarationCall` was likely added to support custom LESS functions in media queries, but it's causing issues with standard media types.

But maybe there's a better fix: we should check if the function name is a known media type. Or we should only try `declarationCall` if the name matches a known LESS function.

Let me check what `declarationCall` is used for. Looking at line 479-513, it parses things like `funcName(property: value)` and creates a `Call` node. This is for custom function calls.

In the context of media features, when would someone write `funcName(property: value)`? It could be a custom LESS function that returns a value to be used in a media query. But the standard media types like `print`, `screen`, `all`, `speech` should not be treated as functions.

I think the fix should be: in `mediaFeature`, we should NOT try `declarationCall` first. Instead, we should try `keyword` first, and only if that fails and the input looks like a function call, try `declarationCall`. But actually, I think the correct approach is to not use `declarationCall` at all in `mediaFeature` since it's not appropriate there.

But let me verify: is `declarationCall` needed in `mediaFeature`? Let me search for any test that uses function calls in media queries:

Both dist and lib have it. But the source also has it. So maybe the issue is that `declarationCall` was added to `mediaFeature` in 4.4.1 (as part of the container queries or layer nesting fixes).

Let me look more carefully. The issue is with `@media print(max-width: 992px)`. The parser sees `print(` and tries `declarationCall`. Let me trace through what happens:

1. `declarationCall` is called
2. It matches `print(` with `parserInput.$re(/^[\w]+\(/)` 
3. `validCall = "print"`
4. Then it tries `this.ruleProperty()` to parse `max-width`
5. Then `this.value()` to parse `992px`
6. Then `parserInput.$char(')')` matches `)`
7. Returns a `Call` node with name `print` and args `[Declaration(max-width, 992px)]`

So the output becomes `@media print(max-width: 992px)` which is invalid CSS.

The fix should be: in `mediaFeature`, we should only try `declarationCall` if we're sure it's not a media type followed by a media feature. Or better yet, we should not use `declarationCall` in `mediaFeature` at all.

But wait, maybe `declarationCall` was added to support something specific. Let me check PR #4342 and #4349.

Actually, let me look at whether the issue is about `declarationCall` being too aggressive. The regex `^[\w]+\(` matches any word followed by `(`. In media queries, media types like `print`, `screen`, `all`, `speech`, `handheld`, `projection`, `tv` etc. could be followed by `(` but they should be treated as media types, not function calls.

The fix could be:
1. Remove `declarationCall` from `mediaFeature` (but this might break something else)
2. Make `declarationCall` check if the name is a known media type
3. Change the order: try `keyword` first, then `declarationCall`

Actually, looking at the parsing more carefully, I think the issue is that `declarationCall` is tried before `keyword`. If we swap the order, `keyword` would match `print` first, then the next iteration would see `(` and go into the `else if` branch to parse the parenthesized expression.

But then `declarationCall` would never be reached. Let me think about when `declarationCall` would be needed in media features...

Actually, I think `declarationCall` was added to support things like custom LESS functions in media queries. But the standard CSS media query syntax doesn't include function calls. So maybe the fix is simply to remove `declarationCall` from the `mediaFeature` function.

const less = require('./packages/less');

// Test various media query formats
const tests = [
    '@media print { .x { color: red; } }',
    '@media print(max-width: 992px) { .main-view { display: flex; } }',
    '@media screen and (max-width: 992px) { .main-view { display: flex; } }',
    '@media print and (max-width: 992px) { .main-view { display: flex; } }',
];

tests.forEach(test => {
    less.render(test, {}, (err, output) => {
        if (err) {
            console.log('ERROR:', test, '->', err.message);
        } else {
            console.log('SUCCESS:', test, '->', output.css);
        }
    });
});
"

Wait, looking at the CSS spec:
- `@media print { }` is valid
- `@media print and (max-width: 992px) { }` is valid
- `@media print(max-width: 992px) { }` is NOT valid CSS

But in LESS, `@media print(max-width: 992px)` could be interpreted as a LESS function call in a media context. However, the correct interpretation should be `print` as a media type and `(max-width: 992px)` as a media feature, which should output `@media print and (max-width: 992px)`.

Now I understand the expected behavior better. Let me look at the CSS output for the media test more carefully. I notice that `@media screen and (min-width: 100px)` is output as `@media screen and (min-width: 100px)`. But there's no test for `@media print(max-width: 992px)` (without `and`).

The key question is: what should `@media print(max-width: 992px)` output?

Given that:
- `@media print` is valid CSS (media type without features)
- `@media (max-width: 992px)` is valid CSS (media feature without type)
- `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` is valid CSS (media type with feature)
- `@media print(max-width: 992px)` is NOT valid CSS

The user probably made a mistake by omitting `and`. But in 4.4.0, this might have been parsed differently and output as `@media print and (max-width: 992px)` or maybe it output `@media print (max-width: 992px)`.

Actually, wait. Let me look at how `mediaFeatures` and the `Expression` handling works. When `mediaFeature` returns an `Expression` with nodes, the `mediaFeatures` collects them. Then in `prepareAndGetNestableAtRule`, these features are passed to the `Media` constructor. Then in `Media.genCSS`, it outputs the features.

But the key is: if `print` is parsed as a keyword and `(max-width: 992px)` is parsed as a `Paren` node, how are they combined? Let me look at how `Expression.genCSS` works:

     1	import Node from './node';
     2	import Paren from './paren';
     3	import Comment from './comment';
     4	import Dimension from './dimension';
     5	import Anonymous from './anonymous';
     6	
     7	const Expression = function(value, noSpacing) {
     8	    this.value = value;
     9	    this.noSpacing = noSpacing;
    10	    if (!value) {
    11	        throw new Error('Expression requires an array parameter');
    12	    }
    13	};
    14	
    15	Expression.prototype = Object.assign(new Node(), {
    16	    type: 'Expression',
    17	
    18	    accept(visitor) {
    19	        this.value = visitor.visitArray(this.value);
    20	    },
    21	
    22	    eval(context) {
    23	        const noSpacing = this.noSpacing;
    24	        let returnValue;
    25	        const mathOn = context.isMathOn();
    26	        const inParenthesis = this.parens;
    27	
    28	        let doubleParen = false;
    29	        if (inParenthesis) {
    30	            context.inParenthesis();
    31	        }
    32	        if (this.value.length > 1) {
    33	            returnValue = new Expression(this.value.map(function (e) {
    34	                if (!e.eval) {
    35	                    return e;
    36	                }
    37	                return e.eval(context);
    38	            }), this.noSpacing);
    39	        } else if (this.value.length === 1) {
    40	            if (this.value[0].parens && !this.value[0].parensInOp && !context.inCalc) {
    41	                doubleParen = true;
    42	            }
    43	            returnValue = this.value[0].eval(context);
    44	        } else {
    45	            returnValue = this;
    46	        }
    47	        if (inParenthesis) {
    48	            context.outOfParenthesis();
    49	        }
    50	        if (this.parens && this.parensInOp && !mathOn && !doubleParen
    51	            && (!(returnValue instanceof Dimension))) {
    52	            returnValue = new Paren(returnValue);
    53	        }
    54	        returnValue.noSpacing = returnValue.noSpacing || noSpacing;
    55	        return returnValue;
    56	    },
    57	
    58	    genCSS(context, output) {
    59	        for (let i = 0; i < this.value.length; i++) {
    60	            this.value[i].genCSS(context, output);
    61	            if (!this.noSpacing && i + 1 < this.value.length) {
    62	                if (i + 1 < this.value.length && !(this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous) ||
    63	                    this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous && this.value[i + 1].value !== ',') {
    64	                    output.add(' ');
    65	                }
    66	            }
    67	        }
    68	    },
    69	
    70	    throwAwayComments() {
    71	        this.value = this.value.filter(function(v) {
    72	            return !(v instanceof Comment);
    73	        });
    74	    }
    75	});
    76	
    77	export default Expression;
    78

Actually, I think the issue might be simpler than I think. Let me re-read the PR description:

> `@media print` throwing an error after upgrading from LESS 4.4.0 to 4.4.1

The user probably has code like:
```less
@media print(max-width: 992px) {
  .main-view {
    display: flex;
  }
}
```

In 4.4.0, this might have been parsed differently (maybe `print` as keyword, `(max-width: 992px)` as paren), and the output might have been `@media print (max-width: 992px)`. But lightningcss might have accepted that (perhaps treating it as equivalent to `@media print and (max-width: 992px)`?).

In 4.4.1, with `declarationCall` added, it now outputs `@media print(max-width: 992px)` (no space, as a function call), which lightningcss rejects.

So the fix should be to remove `declarationCall` from `mediaFeature` or to make it not match when the "function name" is followed by something that looks like a media feature.

But actually, I need to confirm: was `declarationCall` added in 4.4.1? Let me check if there's a way to see the diff between 4.4.0 and 4.4.1.

I see! The container queries use `scroll-state(stuck: top)` syntax. This is exactly why `declarationCall` was added - to support `scroll-state(stuck: top)` in container queries!

So `declarationCall` is needed for container queries like:
```
@container scroll-state(stuck: top) { ... }
```

But it's causing issues for media queries like:
```
@media print(max-width: 992px) { ... }
```

The fix should be to not use `declarationCall` for `@media` but keep it for `@container`. Or better, we should check if the "function name" is a known media type and not treat it as a function call.

Wait, but the `mediaFeature` function is shared between `@media` and `@container`. How can we differentiate?

Looking at the code flow:
- For `@media`: `nestableAtRule` → `prepareAndGetNestableAtRule(tree.Media, index, debugInfo, MediaSyntaxOptions)`
- For `@container`: `nestableAtRule` → `prepareAndGetNestableAtRule(tree.Container, index, debugInfo, ContainerSyntaxOptions)`

Both pass different `syntaxOptions` but currently both have `queryInParens: true`. We could add another option to indicate whether `declarationCall` should be tried.

But actually, looking at the source code more carefully, I think the proper fix is different. The issue is that `declarationCall` matches `print(max-width: 992px)` and treats it as a function call. But the correct interpretation is that `print` is a keyword (media type) and `(max-width: 992px)` is a parenthesized media feature.

The fix should ensure that `declarationCall` only matches when the function name is NOT a known media type. But that's not robust.

Actually, a better fix: in `mediaFeature`, we should first try to match a keyword. If the keyword is followed by `(`, we should check if it's a known media type or a custom function. But we can't know all custom functions.

Hmm, let me think differently. The problem is specifically with `declarationCall` being too aggressive. What if we modify `declarationCall` to require a space before the `(`? No, that would break `scroll-state(stuck: top)`.

Wait, let me look at the `declarationCall` function again:

```javascript
declarationCall: function () {
    let validCall;
    let args;
    const index = parserInput.i;

    parserInput.save();

    validCall = parserInput.$re(/^[\w]+\(/);
    if (!validCall) {
        parserInput.forget();
        return;
    }

    validCall = validCall.substring(0, validCall.length - 1);

    let rule = this.ruleProperty();
    let value;
  
    if (rule) {
        value = this.value();
    }
    
    if (rule && value) {
        args = [new (tree.Declaration)(rule, value, null, null, parserInput.i + currentIndex, fileInfo, true)];
    }

    if (!parserInput.$char(')')) {
        parserInput.restore('Could not parse call arguments or missing \')\'');
        return;
    }

    parserInput.forget();

    return new(tree.Call)(validCall, args, index + currentIndex, fileInfo);
},
```

This function:
1. Matches `word(`
2. Tries to parse a rule property (like `max-width`) 
3. If rule found, tries to parse a value (like `992px`)
4. If both found, creates args
5. Expects `)`
6. Returns a `Call` node

For `print(max-width: 992px)`:
- Matches `print(`
- `rule` = `max-width` (parsed as a property)
- `value` = `992px`
- `args` = `[Declaration(max-width, 992px)]`
- `)` matches
- Returns `Call("print", [Declaration...])`

The problem is that `ruleProperty()` matches `max-width` and `value()` matches `992px`. So `declarationCall` successfully parses `print(max-width: 992px)` as a function call.

But wait, what if `declarationCall` requires that the function name NOT be immediately followed by something that looks like a media feature? That's tricky.

Alternative approach: maybe we should check in `mediaFeature` that if `declarationCall` matches, we should verify that the function name is not a known media type. But media types can be custom (like `a` in the test file).

Actually, I think the simplest and correct fix is: in `mediaFeature`, we should try `keyword` first, and only if that fails, try `declarationCall`. But wait, if we try `keyword` first, it would match `print` and then on the next iteration, the parser would see `(` and go into the `else if` branch, parsing `(max-width: 992px)` as a parenthesized expression. But what about `scroll-state(stuck: top)`? 

For `scroll-state(stuck: top)`:
- `keyword` would match `scroll-state`? Let me check: `keyword` matches `[\w-]+` or similar. Yes, `scroll-state` would be matched as a keyword.
- Then `(` would be matched by the `else if` branch
- Inside the parens, `stuck: top` would be parsed as a property/value pair
- This would create a `Paren` containing a `Declaration`
- The expression would be `[keyword("scroll-state"), Paren(Declaration("stuck", "top"))]`
- Output: `scroll-state (stuck: top)` (with a space)

But the expected output for `@container scroll-state(stuck: top)` is `scroll-state(stuck: top)` without a space. So removing `declarationCall` would break container queries!

So we need a different approach. Let me think...

Actually, looking at the `declarationCall` function more carefully: it parses `word(property: value)` and returns a `Call` node. The `Call.genCSS` outputs `word(property: value)`. This is exactly what we want for container queries like `scroll-state(stuck: top)`.

For media queries, `declarationCall` should NOT match `print(max-width: 992px)` because `print` is a media type, not a function. But how do we distinguish?

One approach: in `mediaFeature`, after `declarationCall` succeeds, check if the resulting node makes sense in context. But that's complex.

Another approach: modify `declarationCall` to check if the function name is a "known" function or follows a pattern that distinguishes it from media types. But that's fragile.

Actually, I think the best approach is: in `mediaFeature`, we should first try to match the pattern manually: if we see `word(` where the word is not followed by a colon (i.e., it's not a property:value pair), we should treat it as a keyword + paren, not as a function call.

Wait, let me look at the container query syntax: `scroll-state(stuck: top)`. The argument is `stuck: top` which is a property:value declaration. For `print(max-width: 992px)`, the argument is also `max-width: 992px` which is also a property:value declaration.

So both look like function calls! The difference is that `print` is a media type and `scroll-state` is a container query function.

How did this work before `declarationCall` was added? Before 4.4.1, `mediaFeature` had:
```javascript
e = entities.keyword() || entities.variable() || entities.mixinLookup()
```

So `scroll-state` would be parsed as a keyword, and then `(stuck: top)` would be parsed as a parenthesized declaration. The output would be `scroll-state (stuck: top)` (with a space).

But the expected CSS output for container queries is `scroll-state(stuck: top)` (without space). So `declarationCall` was added to fix this - to make `scroll-state(stuck: top)` output without a space.

However, this broke media queries because `print(max-width: 992px)` is now also parsed as a function call.

The fix should differentiate between media queries and container queries. In media queries, `print` is a media type and should be treated as keyword + paren. In container queries, `scroll-state` is a container query function and should be treated as a function call.

But how? The `mediaFeature` function receives `syntaxOptions`. Currently both `MediaSyntaxOptions` and `ContainerSyntaxOptions` have `queryInParens: true`. We could add another option like `allowDeclarationCall` that is true for container queries but false for media queries.

Let me check if there are any other differences between how media and container queries should be parsed:

```javascript

```

When `p && e` (both property and value found), it creates a `Paren` with a `Declaration` inside. And if `!spacing`, it sets `noSpacing = true` on the `Paren` node.

The `spacing` flag is set by checking if there's a word before the `(`:
```javascript
if (parserInput.$re(/^[0-9a-z-]*\s+\(/)) {
    spacing = true;
}
```

For `print(max-width: 992px)`:
1. First iteration: `keyword` matches `print`, nodes = [`print`]
2. Second iteration: `keyword` fails, `(` is matched
   - `p = this.property()` matches `max-width`
   - `e = this.value()` matches `992px`
   - `)` matches
   - Since `p && e`, creates `Paren(Declaration(...))`
   - `spacing` is false (because `print` is followed directly by `(`)
   - So `noSpacing = true` on the Paren node
3. Result: `Expression([keyword("print"), Paren(Declaration("max-width", "992px"))])`

Now in `Expression.genCSS`, when `noSpacing` is true on a node, the space between nodes is suppressed. Let me check how `genCSS` handles `noSpacing`:

Looking at `Expression.genCSS`:
```javascript
genCSS(context, output) {
    for (let i = 0; i < this.value.length; i++) {
        this.value[i].genCSS(context, output);
        if (!this.noSpacing && i + 1 < this.value.length) {
            if (i + 1 < this.value.length && !(this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous) ||
                this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous && this.value[i + 1].value !== ',') {
                output.add(' ');
            }
        }
    }
},
```

This checks `this.noSpacing` (on the Expression itself), not on individual nodes. So the `noSpacing` on the `Paren` node is not used here.

- Check spacing: now the remaining input is `(max-width: 992px)`. The regex `^[0-9a-z-]*\s+\(` tries to match `(max-width: 992px)`. It matches `(` (since `[0-9a-z-]*` matches zero characters, and `\s+` requires at least one whitespace, but there's no whitespace before `(`). Wait, actually `[0-9a-z-]*` matches empty string, then `\s+` needs whitespace. There's no whitespace before `(`, so the regex fails. `spacing` remains true from the first iteration.

But wait, `spacing` is not reset to false before the second iteration! It's only set at the beginning of `mediaFeature` and then conditionally set to true. So once it's true, it stays true.

Then in the second iteration, after matching `(` and the content:
```javascript
if (!spacing) {
    nodes[nodes.length - 1].noSpacing = true;
}
```

Since `spacing` is true (from the first iteration), `noSpacing` is NOT set. Good.

But the output is still `print(max-width: 992px)` without space. Let me check the `Expression.genCSS` more carefully...

Actually, wait. Let me look at `Expression.genCSS` again:

```javascript
genCSS(context, output) {
    for (let i = 0; i < this.value.length; i++) {
        this.value[i].genCSS(context, output);
        if (!this.noSpacing && i + 1 < this.value.length) {
            if (i + 1 < this.value.length && !(this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous) ||
                this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous && this.value[i + 1].value !== ',') {
                output.add(' ');
            }
        }
    }
},
```

For nodes `[keyword("print"), Paren(Declaration("max-width", "992px"))]`:
- i=0: `keyword.genCSS` outputs `print`
- Check: `!this.noSpacing` (true) && `i+1 < length` (true)
- Inner check: `i+1 < length` (true) && `!(Paren instanceof Anonymous)` → true
- So it adds a space!
- i=1: `Paren.genCSS` outputs `(max-width: 992px)`

So the output should be `print (max-width: 992px)` with a space. But the test shows `print(max-width: 992px)` without space!

Something else must be going on. Let me check if the `noSpacing` on the `Paren` node affects the `Expression.genCSS`...

Actually, wait. Let me look at the `Paren.genCSS` and see if it has a `noSpacing` property that affects how `Expression` handles it. Looking at `Expression.genCSS`:

```javascript
if (!this.noSpacing && i + 1 < this.value.length) {
    if (i + 1 < this.value.length && !(this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous) ||
        this.value[i + 1] instanceof Anonymous && this.value[i + 1].value !== ',') {
        output.add(' ');
    }
}
```

This checks `this.noSpacing` (the Expression's own noSpacing). But maybe `this.noSpacing` is being set from somewhere? Let me check the `Expression` constructor: `const Expression = function(value, noSpacing) { this.value = value; this.noSpacing = noSpacing; ... }`.

In `mediaFeature`, the expression is created as `new(tree.Expression)(nodes)` without a second argument. So `noSpacing` is `undefined`.

But wait! Let me check what happens when the `Expression` is evaluated. In `Expression.eval`:

```javascript
eval(context) {
    ...
    if (this.value.length > 1) {
        returnValue = new Expression(this.value.map(...), this.noSpacing);
    } else if (this.value.length === 1) {
        ...
    }
    ...
    returnValue.noSpacing = returnValue.noSpacing || noSpacing;
    return returnValue;
}
```

If `this.value.length === 2`, it creates a new `Expression` with `this.noSpacing` which is `undefined`. So `noSpacing` remains `undefined`.

Hmm, let me actually trace through the code by adding some debug output. But first, let me check if maybe the issue is that `Expression.eval` returns something else entirely.

Let me check `Expression.eval` again:

```javascript
eval(context) {
    const noSpacing = this.noSpacing;
    let returnValue;
    const mathOn = context.isMathOn();
    const inParenthesis = this.parens;

    let doubleParen = false;
    if (inParenthesis) {
        context.inParenthesis();
    }
    if (this.value.length > 1) {
        returnValue = new Expression(this.value.map(function (e) {
            if (!e.eval) {
                return e;
            }
            return e.eval(context);
        }), this.noSpacing);
    } else if (this.value.length === 1) {
        if (this.value[0].parens && !this.value[0].parensInOp && !context.inCalc) {
            doubleParen = true;
        }
        returnValue = this.value[0].eval(context);
    } else {
        returnValue = this;
    }
    if (inParenthesis) {
        context.outOfParenthesis();
    }
    if (this.parens && this.parensInOp && !mathOn && !doubleParen
        && (!(returnValue instanceof Dimension))) {
        returnValue = new Paren(returnValue);
    }
    returnValue.noSpacing = returnValue.noSpacing || noSpacing;
    return returnValue;
},
```

On line 33, if `this.value.length > 1`, it maps each value through `eval` and creates a new `Expression` with `this.noSpacing`. But wait, it does `this.value.map(function (e) { return e.eval(context); })`. Each node in the value array is evaluated.

`Keyword` doesn't have an `eval` method, so it inherits from `Node` which probably just returns `this`.

Now let me check `Paren.eval`:

```javascript
eval(context) {
    const paren = new Paren(this.value.eval(context));
   
    if (this.noSpacing) {
        paren.noSpacing = true;
    }

    return paren;
}
```

So `Paren.eval` creates a new `Paren` and preserves `noSpacing`. But inside `mediaFeature`, when we create the `Paren`, we set `noSpacing = true` (if `!spacing`). So after eval, the `Paren` still has `noSpacing = true`.

But how does `noSpacing` on a `Paren` affect the `Expression`'s output? The `Expression.genCSS` doesn't check child nodes' `noSpacing`. 

Wait, maybe the issue is that `Expression.eval` returns something other than an `Expression`. Let me check: if `this.value.length === 1`, it returns `this.value[0].eval(context)`. But in our case, `this.value.length` is 2 (keyword + paren), so it goes to the first branch and creates a new `Expression`.

But hold on, let me check if maybe the problem is earlier. Let me check what `Media.genCSS` does:

```javascript
genCSS(context, output) {
    output.add('@media ', this._fileInfo, this._index);
    this.features.genCSS(context, output);
    this.outputRuleset(context, output, this.rules);
},
```

So `this.features.genCSS(context, output)` is called. `this.features` is a `Value` (set in the constructor: `this.features = new Value(features)`). After `eval`, `media.features = this.features.eval(context)`. If the original `Value` has one element (the `Expression`), `Value.eval` returns `Expression.eval(context)` which should return an `Expression`.

So `media.features` is an `Expression`. Then `Expression.genCSS` is called. This should output with spaces.

     1	import Node from './node';
     2	import Value from './value';
     3	import Keyword from './keyword';
     4	import Anonymous from './anonymous';
     5	import * as Constants from '../constants';
     6	const MATH = Constants.Math;
     7	
     8	function evalName(context, name) {
     9	    let value = '';
    10	    let i;
    11	    const n = name.length;
    12	    const output = {add: function (s) {value += s;}};
    13	    for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    14	        name[i].eval(context).genCSS(context, output);
    15	    }
    16	    return value;
    17	}
    18	
    19	const Declaration = function(name, value, important, merge, index, currentFileInfo, inline, variable) {
    20	    this.name = name;
    21	    this.value = (value instanceof Node) ? value : new Value([value ? new Anonymous(value) : null]);
    22	    this.important = important ? ` ${important.trim()}` : '';
    23	    this.merge = merge;
    24	    this._index = index;
    25	    this._fileInfo = currentFileInfo;
    26	    this.inline = inline || false;
    27	    this.variable = (variable !== undefined) ? variable
    28	        : (name.charAt && (name.charAt(0) === '@'));
    29	    this.allowRoot = true;
    30	    this.setParent(this.value, this);
    31	};
    32	
    33	Declaration.prototype = Object.assign(new Node(), {
    34	    type: 'Declaration',
    35	
    36	    genCSS(context, output) {
    37	        output.add(this.name + (context.compress ? ':' : ': '), this.fileInfo(), this.getIndex());
    38	        try {
    39	            this.value.genCSS(context, output);
    40	        }
    41	        catch (e) {
    42	            e.index = this._index;
    43	            e.filename = this._fileInfo.filename;
    44	            throw e;
    45	        }
    46	        output.add(this.important + ((this.inline || (context.lastRule && context.compress)) ? '' : ';'), this._fileInfo, this._index);
    47	    },
    48	
    49	    eval(context) {
    50	        let mathBypass = false, prevMath, name = this.name, evaldValue, variable = this.variable;
    51	        if (typeof name !== 'string') {
    52	            // expand 'primitive' name directly to get
    53	            // things faster (~10% for benchmark.less):
    54	            name = (name.length === 1) && (name[0] instanceof Keyword) ?
    55	                name[0].value : evalName(context, name);
    56	            variable = false; // never treat expanded interpolation as new variable name
    57	        }
    58	
    59	        // @todo remove when parens-division is default
    60	        if (name === 'font' && context.math === MATH.ALWAYS) {
    61	            mathBypass = true;
    62	            prevMath = context.math;
    63	            context.math = MATH.PARENS_DIVISION;
    64	        }
    65	        try {
    66	            context.importantScope.push({});
    67	            evaldValue = this.value.eval(context);
    68	
    69	            if (!this.variable && evaldValue.type === 'DetachedRuleset') {
    70	                throw { message: 'Rulesets cannot be evaluated on a property.',
    71	                    index: this.getIndex(), filename: this.fileInfo().filename };
    72	            }
    73	            let important = this.important;
    74	            const importantResult = context.importantScope.pop();
    75	            if (!important && importantResult.important) {
    76	                important = importantResult.important;
    77	            }
    78	
    79	            return new Declaration(name,
    80	                evaldValue,
    81	                important,
    82	                this.merge,
    83	                this.getIndex(), this.fileInfo(), this.inline,
    84	                variable);
    85	        }
    86	        catch (e) {
    87	            if (typeof e.index !== 'number') {
    88	                e.index = this.getIndex();
    89	                e.filename = this.fileInfo().filename;
    90	            }
    91	            throw e;
    92	        }
    93	        finally {
    94	            if (mathBypass) {
    95	                context.math = prevMath;
    96	            }
    97	        }
    98	    },
    99	
   100	    makeImportant() {
   101	        return new Declaration(this.name,
   102	            this.value,
   103	            '!important',
   104	            this.merge,
   105	            this.getIndex(), this.fileInfo(), this.inline);
   106	    }
   107	});
   108	
   109	export default Declaration;

    22	import parseVersion from 'parse-node-version';
    23	
    24	export default function(environment, fileManagers) {
    25	    let sourceMapOutput, sourceMapBuilder, parseTree, importManager;
    26	
    27	    environment = new Environment(environment, fileManagers);
    28	    sourceMapOutput = SourceMapOutput(environment);
    29	    sourceMapBuilder = SourceMapBuilder(sourceMapOutput, environment);
    30	    parseTree = ParseTree(sourceMapBuilder);
    31	    importManager = ImportManager(environment);
    32	
    33	    const render = Render(environment, parseTree, importManager);
    34	    const parse = Parse(environment, parseTree, importManager);
    35	
    36	    const v = parseVersion(`v${version}`);
    37	    const initial = {
    38	        version: [v.major, v.minor, v.patch],
    39	        data,
    40	        tree,
    41	        Environment,
    42	        AbstractFileManager,
    43	        AbstractPluginLoader,
    44	        environment,
    45	        visitors,
    46	        Parser,
    47	        functions: functions(environment),
    48	        contexts,
    49	        SourceMapOutput: sourceMapOutput,

    53	        render,

    55	        LessError,
    56	        transformTree,
    57	        utils,
    58	        PluginManager,
    59	        logger
    60	    };
    61	
    62	    // Create a public API
    63	
    64	    const ctor = function(t) {
    65	        return function() {
    66	            const obj = Object.create(t.prototype);
    67	            t.apply(obj, Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments, 0));
    68	            return obj;
    69	        };
    70	    };
    71	    let t;
    72	    const api = Object.create(initial);
    73	    for (const n in initial.tree) {
    74	        /* eslint guard-for-in: 0 */
    75	        t = initial.tree[n];
    76	        if (typeof t === 'function') {
    77	            api[n.toLowerCase()] = ctor(t);
    78	        }
    79	        else {
    80	            api[n] = Object.create(null);
    81	            for (const o in t) {
    82	                /* eslint guard-for-in: 0 */
    83	                api[n][o.toLowerCase()] = ctor(t[o]);
    84	            }
    85	        }
    86	    }
    87	
    88	    /**
    89	     * Some of the functions assume a `this` context of the API object,
    90	     * which causes it to fail when wrapped for ES6 imports.
    91	     * 
    92	     * An assumed `this` should be removed in the future.
    93	     */
    94	    initial.parse = initial.parse.bind(api);
    95	    initial.render = initial.render.bind(api);
    96	
    97	    return api;
    98	}

     1	import * as utils from './utils';
     2	
     3	export default function(environment, ParseTree) {
     4	    const render = function (input, options, callback) {
     5	        if (typeof options === 'function') {
     6	            callback = options;
     7	            options = utils.copyOptions(this.options, {});
     8	        }
     9	        else {
    10	            options = utils.copyOptions(this.options, options || {});
    11	        }
    12	
    13	        if (!callback) {
    14	            const self = this;
    15	            return new Promise(function (resolve, reject) {
    16	                render.call(self, input, options, function(err, output) {
    17	                    if (err) {
    18	                        reject(err);
    19	                    } else {
    20	                        resolve(output);
    21	                    }
    22	                });
    23	            });
    24	        } else {
    25	            this.parse(input, options, function(err, root, imports, options) {
    26	                if (err) { return callback(err); }
    27	
    28	                let result;
    29	                try {
    30	                    const parseTree = new ParseTree(root, imports);
    31	                    result = parseTree.toCSS(options);
    32	                }
    33	                catch (err) { return callback(err); }
    34	
    35	                callback(null, result);
    36	            });
    37	        }
    38	    };
    39	
    40	    return render;
    41	}

Here's the result of running `cat -n` on /testbed/packages/less/src/less/parse-tree.js:
     1	import LessError from './less-error';
     2	import transformTree from './transform-tree';
     3	import logger from './logger';
     4	
     5	export default function(SourceMapBuilder) {
     6	    class ParseTree {
     7	        constructor(root, imports) {
     8	            this.root = root;
     9	            this.imports = imports;
    10	        }
    11	
    12	        toCSS(options) {
    13	            let evaldRoot;
    14	            const result = {};
    15	            let sourceMapBuilder;
    16	            try {
    17	                evaldRoot = transformTree(this.root, options);
    18	            } catch (e) {
    19	                throw new LessError(e, this.imports);
    20	            }
    21	
    22	            try {
    23	                const compress = Boolean(options.compress);
    24	                if (compress) {
    25	                    logger.warn('The compress option has been deprecated. ' + 
    26	                        'We recommend you use a dedicated css minifier, for instance see less-plugin-clean-css.');
    27	                }
    28	
    29	                const toCSSOptions = {
    30	                    compress,
    31	                    dumpLineNumbers: options.dumpLineNumbers,
    32	                    strictUnits: Boolean(options.strictUnits),
    33	                    numPrecision: 8};
    34	
    35	                if (options.sourceMap) {
    36	                    sourceMapBuilder = new SourceMapBuilder(options.sourceMap);
    37	                    result.css = sourceMapBuilder.toCSS(evaldRoot, toCSSOptions, this.imports);
    38	                } else {
    39	                    result.css = evaldRoot.toCSS(toCSSOptions);
    40	                }
    41	            } catch (e) {
    42	                throw new LessError(e, this.imports);
    43	            }
    44	
    45	            if (options.pluginManager) {
    46	                const postProcessors = options.pluginManager.getPostProcessors();
    47	                for (let i = 0; i < postProcessors.length; i++) {
    48	                    result.css = postProcessors[i].process(result.css, { sourceMap: sourceMapBuilder, options, imports: this.imports });
    49	                }
    50	            }
    51	            if (options.sourceMap) {
    52	                result.map = sourceMapBuilder.getExternalSourceMap();
    53	            }
    54	
    55	            result.imports = [];
    56	            for (const file in this.imports.files) {
    57	                if (Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(this.imports.files, file) && file !== this.imports.rootFilename) {
    58	                    result.imports.push(file);
    59	                }
    60	            }
    61	            return result;
    62	        }
    63	    }
    64	
    65	    return ParseTree;
    66	}
    67
